characters.physical_details: Medium build, strong hands from domestic labor, dark hair she keeps tied back. A face that defaults to competence — people read her as calm. A scar on her left wrist from a childhood burn she does not discuss.

key_relationships.deshi_mera: mother-in-law — adversarial, mutually dependent, occasionally tender
key_relationships.rimi_mera: daughter (12) — old enough to sense something is wrong
key_relationships.kael_mera: son (8) — still young enough to be managed
key_relationships.asha_mera: daughter (4) — the child who manifests first
key_relationships.jin_parra: stranger who becomes confidant through shared shame (Chapter 16)
key_relationships.nessa_okoye: stranger who becomes friend through shared fear (Chapter 8)

characters.occupation_status: No occupation stated; she is the functional head of the Mera family in her husband's absence.
characters.literacy_languages: Literate; speaks the common language and a regional dialect (the dialect of the lullaby Kiri later hums).

state_transitions.change: Arrives at shrine carrying secret of husband's departure
Asha's manifestation forces Suna to confront the uncanny
Befriends Nessa through mutual fear; still hiding her own secret
Learns Jin's parallel lie; decides to tell the truth
Declares husband's absence publicly; leaves with Jin and Melie alongside her family

characters.availability: Present in all chapters; POV in chapters 1, 4, 8, 16, 18.

characters.physical_details: Lean, younger-looking than 29, slightly hunched posture from carrying Melie. A speech hesitation — a brief jaw-lock before certain consonants — that he has had since adolescence. Dark-circled eyes from poor sleep.

key_relationships.melie_parra: daughter (6) — his entire world and the source of his deepest fear
key_relationships.bett_dao: stranger who saves Melie during allergic reaction; becomes a practical ally
key_relationships.bram_okoye: stranger who offers silent kindness; becomes an ally
key_relationships.suna_mera: stranger who shares his exact lie; becomes his closest confidant

characters.occupation_status: Unspecified manual worker; currently traveling to bring Melie to her mother's family.

state_transitions.change: Declares wife as deceased in first naming
Melie's allergic reaction creates dependency on Bett Dao's knowledge
Naming interrogation cracks his story publicly; walks out
Witnesses Gitta's confession; feels his own lie tremble
Confesses truth to Suna privately
Declares wife alive in the final naming; Melie has been told offscreen between chapters 17-18

characters.availability: Present in all chapters; POV in chapters 6, 10, 14.

characters.physical_details: Mid-forties, tall, angular face, hands roughened by shrine maintenance. Carries herself with a stillness that reads as authority. Her voice is her most distinctive feature — measured, resonant, with the cadence of someone who speaks ritual language daily.

key_relationships.olet: head custodian — her superior and occasional philosophical opponent
key_relationships.suna_mera: the family member she engages with most seriously
key_relationships.bram_okoye: the most articulate challenger of her practice

characters.occupation_status: Custodian of the transit shrine for twenty years; conducts the naming ritual.
characters.literacy_languages: Literate; speaks the common language and the shrine's ritual language.

state_transitions.change: Privately troubled by the coercive dimension of the naming she believes in
Recognizes she pushed Jin too hard
Perceives the absence-pattern; confronts the ethical cost of what the naming demands
Accepts the families' imperfect, honest map as complete

characters.availability: Present in all chapters; POV in chapters 2, 9, 12, 15.

characters.physical_details: Lanky, still growing, hair in braids. Observant eyes that she hides behind performative indifference. Bites her nails. Carries a journal she writes in nightly.

key_relationships.bram_okoye: father — she admires his principles and watches them fail
key_relationships.nessa_okoye: mother — tender but frightened; Leni protects her from seeing how scared Leni is
key_relationships.tomas_okoye: brother (10) — his manifestation of Jin's speech pattern frightens her
key_relationships.ada_okoye: baby sister (1) — Leni helps care for her

characters.literacy_languages: Literate; speaks the common language. Writes daily in her journal.

state_transitions.change: Established as the novel's sharpest observer
Watches her father lose a principled fight
Discovers Arash-Bett connection; sees the shrine's nighttime reality
Her journal crystallizes the families' collective reckoning

characters.availability: Present in all chapters; POV in chapters 3, 7, 11, 17.

characters.physical_details: Broad-shouldered, thick-necked, a physical presence that matches his vocal authority. Graying at the temples. His face is expressive when he does not control it — anger, fear, and pride all readable.

key_relationships.tila_kondo: wife (49) — supportive, practical, keeps the Kondo household running
key_relationships.arash_kondo: son (27) — traveling to his own wedding; his divergence is Venn's deepest wound
key_relationships.mava_kondo: aunt (73) — the elder whose gesture Asha manifests
key_relationships.ori_kondo: niece (9) — the child who manifests water terror

characters.occupation_status: Patriarch of the Kondo family; his occupation is the family itself.

state_transitions.change: His resistance to the Mera connection is overridden by Deshi and Mava
Confronts Bett; Arash's hesitation cracks his authority
Leads a diminished family down the road after Arash refuses to continue to his wedding

characters.availability: Present in all chapters; POV in chapters 5, 13.

characters.physical_details: Small, thin, careful movements. White hair, deeply lined face. Hands that shake under extreme stress but are otherwise steady. A particular prayer gesture — hands clasped, thumbs circling — that Pik Leo later manifests.

key_relationships.fen_tesh: grandson (16) — not biologically hers; taken as an infant from a dead family
key_relationships.miro_tesh: son (45) — believes Fen is a relative's child

characters.occupation_status: Retired; the eldest Tesh. Her occupation for sixteen years has been maintaining the lie about Fen.
characters.literacy_languages: Literate; speaks the common language and possibly an older regional dialect.

state_transitions.change: Declares Fen as her grandson without a flicker — the smoothest lie
Remembers a recipe she has never made; unsettled by her own knowledge
Hands shake during the chaotic naming; her secret becomes unbearable
Confesses Fen's true origins publicly
Declares the truth from the start; Fen chooses her

characters.availability: Present in all chapters; never POV but central to chapters 14 and 18.

characters.physical_details: Strong, compact build. Weathered skin. Moves with physical competence — she is the person you want in a medical emergency. Her face is guarded in public, more open in private.

key_relationships.hala_dao: sister (44) — Hala is protective of Bett, sometimes to the point of control
key_relationships.arash_kondo: growing intimate connection that crosses family lines
key_relationships.jin_parra: practical ally after saving Melie; shares the experience of loss
key_relationships.nori_dao: daughter (7) — not biological; taken in after Ren drowned

characters.occupation_status: Unspecified; she is defined by what she lost (son Ren, drowned at 3) and what she does (takes in Nori, forms bond with Arash).

state_transitions.change: Saves Melie during allergic reaction; practical bond with Jin formed
Nighttime connection with Arash observed by Leni
Names her drowned son Ren publicly after Venn's confrontation
Names Ren in the final naming; Arash walks toward her after the ceremony

characters.physical_details: Mid-sixties, lean, unhurried in movement. His face reveals nothing under pressure. His most distinctive quality is consistency — he does not change register regardless of what is happening around him.

key_relationships.fasa: subordinate custodian — he trusts her practice but adds metaphysical claims she does not make

characters.occupation_status: Head custodian of the transit shrine.
characters.literacy_languages: Literate; speaks the common language and the shrine's ritual language.

state_transitions.change: Reveals his belief that the flood is the shrine holding the families; the road will not open until the naming is done
Tells Fasa the losses must be named, not just the connections

characters.availability: Present at the shrine throughout; most active in chapters 1, 7, 12, 15, 18.

characters.physical_details: Lean, angular, moves with the deliberate economy of someone who has been managing a household for decades. Sharp eyes. Her posture communicates judgment before she speaks.

key_relationships.suna_mera: daughter-in-law — complex: adversarial, dependent, possibly aware of son's departure
key_relationships.peyam_mera: brother (72) — elderly, devout, her closest family peer

characters.occupation_status: Retired matriarch of the Mera family.
characters.literacy_languages: Literate; speaks the common language and uses traditional sayings as conversational weapons.

state_transitions.change: Surprises everyone by engaging with Mava during the second naming
Closes her eyes but does not contradict when Suna names the absent husband

characters.physical_details: Tall, thin, glasses. The posture of a teacher — slightly forward, engaged, ready to respond. His hands move when he argues.

key_relationships.nessa_okoye: wife (41) — she is quieter and more frightened than he is
key_relationships.leni_okoye: daughter (15) — she watches him with admiration and the dawning recognition that being right is not enough
key_relationships.jin_parra: ally formed through shared cigarette and silence (Chapter 10)

characters.occupation_status: Former teacher; currently fleeing the political crisis with his family.
characters.literacy_languages: Literate; educated; speaks the common language with a teacher's precision.

state_transitions.change: Leads resistance to the naming; loses to the custodians' material leverage
Offers silent kindness to Jin after the naming humiliation
Names his dead son Efe in the final naming

characters.physical_details: Shorter than Bram, rounder, a face that shows fear more readily than she would like. She holds baby Ada against her chest as a kind of shield.

key_relationships.suna_mera: friend formed through shared fear of the manifestations (Chapter 8)

characters.occupation_status: Mother; her identity at the shrine is entirely maternal.

state_transitions.change: Reveals to Suna that she and Bram lost a son (Efe) before Tomas was born

characters.physical_details: Tall for his age, dark-haired, quiet. Resembles no one in the Tesh family, a fact no one has remarked on aloud.

key_relationships.gitta_tesh: grandmother (not biologically) — the only parent figure whose claim on him is chosen rather than given

state_transitions.change: Learns his identity is constructed; leaves the hall in shock
Returns to sit beside Gitta — choosing her
Declares 'I am her grandson' — chosen family affirmed publicly

characters.availability: Present in all chapters; most active in chapters 14, 18.

characters.physical_details: Physically similar to Venn but leaner, younger, less rigid in posture. His face shows conflict he cannot hide.

key_relationships.venn_kondo: father — whose authority he is in the process of outgrowing
key_relationships.bett_dao: growing intimate connection discovered by Leni in Chapter 11

characters.occupation_status: Venn's son; traveling to his own wedding. His bride-to-be is at the destination, not at the shrine.

state_transitions.change: Nighttime connection with Bett observed by Leni
Hesitates visibly between Venn and Bett during the confrontation
Declares he will not continue to his wedding; walks toward Bett after the naming

characters.physical_details: Frail, white-haired, constantly rubs her left ear with her palm in a slow circular motion. This gesture is the first trait Asha manifests.

key_relationships.ori_kondo: great-niece — uses a specific endearment for her

characters.occupation_status: Elderly aunt in the Kondo family.
characters.literacy_languages: Speaks the common language and an old regional dialect that includes the endearment Asha later uses.

state_transitions.change: Shocked when Suna asks about the endearment; reveals it belonged to her dead sister
Weeps during the second naming; her grief makes the Mera-Kondo connection tangible

characters.physical_details: Average build, careful in movement, watchful. His anxiety about the naming is visible in how he positions himself — slightly in front of Wynn and Pik.

key_relationships.pik_leo: adopted son (7) — origin unknown; adopted from an institution with possibly falsified records

characters.occupation_status: Unspecified; defined at the shrine by his role as Pik's father and the vulnerability of their non-traditional family structure.

state_transitions.change: Struggles to describe his family in the naming's traditional categories
Pik manifests Gitta's prayer gesture; Cade's anxiety about Pik's unknown origins intensifies
Asks what the naming means for a child whose origins are unknown
Declares Pik as his son without apology

characters.physical_details: Sturdy, protective posture — she positions herself between Bett and threats. Her face is more openly emotional than Bett's.

key_relationships.bett_dao: younger sister — fiercely protective, sometimes controlling

characters.occupation_status: Eldest Dao sibling; functional head of the Dao family.

state_transitions.change: Steps between Venn and Bett during the confrontation
Argues that the connections are real regardless of the custodians' theory

timeline.story_start: Late monsoon season, contemporary period. The exact year is unspecified. The political crisis is current and ongoing.

seasonal_track.season: Late monsoon — heavy intermittent rain, high humidity, lush green landscape, the flood sustained by continued rainfall and upstream damage from the political crisis.
Monsoon breaking — rain stops by Day 19, skies clear for the first time, the flood begins to recede. Stars visible for the first time in Chapter 16.

key_events.event: Flood cuts off roads; families converge on shrine
Daily routines established; children's play group forms
First manifestation: Asha manifests Mava's gesture and vocabulary
Second naming; Deshi engages with Mava
Bram's resistance; custodians reveal food-access leverage
Tomas manifests Jin's speech pattern; Kiri hums Mera lullaby
Communal meal; Deshi and Hala discover matching techniques
Naming interrogation cracks Jin's story; he walks out
Pik manifests Gitta's prayer gesture; Olet claims the flood is the shrine holding them
Ori's water terror; Venn confronts Bett; communal rupture
Jin tells Suna the truth; rain stops; stars visible
News arrives: road will reopen; communal debate

geography.primary_setting: A hilltop transit shrine at the convergence of seven roads, in a mountainous monsoon region. The shrine overlooks a valley floor now submerged by flooding. The landscape is lush, green, and threatening — monsoon beauty.

key_locations.name: Main hall
key_locations.details: The shrine's largest enclosed space. Stone walls, open on one side to the valley view. Where the naming takes place. Large enough for all 48 people (43 family members + 5 custodians). Oil lamps. Stone floor.

key_locations.name: Sleeping quarters
key_locations.details: Divided sections off the main hall and in adjacent stone structures. Assigned by the custodians. Mats on stone floors. Thin partition walls. Sound carries between sections.

key_locations.name: Kitchen area
key_locations.details: Outdoor, covered by a stone overhang. Cooking fire. Communal pots. Food stores controlled by the custodians. Where the collaborative meals happen.

key_locations.name: Prayer courtyard
key_locations.details: Open-air courtyard at the center of the compound. Stone-paved. Where Peyam prays alone, where Arash and Bett meet at night, where Jin and Suna have their Chapter 16 conversation.

key_locations.name: Custodians' quarters
key_locations.details: Off-limits to families. Contains the radio. Olet and Fasa live here with three assistant custodians.

key_locations.name: Cistern
key_locations.details: Spring-fed water supply. Stone basin. Where Jin washes Melie's hair. Where Fasa washes her face in Chapter 15.

key_locations.name: Terraced garden
key_locations.details: Small cultivated area on the hillside. Herbs and some vegetables. Supplementary food source.

key_locations.name: Road down
key_locations.details: A switchback path from the shrine to the valley floor. About a 15-minute descent when dry. Currently impassable — the lower sections are submerged. Visible from the main hall's open side.

key_locations.name: Waterline
key_locations.details: The visible boundary of the flood. Brown water covering the valley floor and the lower road. The sound of the flood is always audible from the shrine — a steady rush that intensifies during rain.

distances: The shrine sits approximately 200 meters above the valley floor.
The road from shrine to waterline is a switchback path — 15 minutes to descend when dry.
The seven roads converge at the base of the hill; all are submerged at the valley level.
The compound is compact — main hall, sleeping quarters, kitchen, and courtyard are all within 50 meters of each other.
The custodians' quarters are separated from the main compound by a short covered walkway.

world_rules: The naming is the shrine's central rite, performed for any group that stays more than one night. It is a tradition specific to this shrine, not universally practiced.
Participation in the naming is the condition for access to the shrine's common food stores. Families who refuse may stay but must provide for themselves.
The custodians have lived at the shrine permanently and maintain it. There are five: Olet (head), Fasa (naming lead), and three assistants.
The shrine has no electric power. Lighting is by oil lamp. The custodians have a battery-powered radio.
The political crisis involves a nationalist movement tightening identity registration, ethnic documentation, and movement permits. This has diverted road crews and emergency services.

The flood is seasonal (monsoon) but has been prolonged by upstream damage from the political crisis (destroyed infrastructure, no maintenance). Olet claims the road does not reopen until the naming is complete — this claim is metaphysical and is neither confirmed nor denied by the novel.

The children's manifestations are real phenomena — allergies, gestures, speech patterns, fears — that cannot be explained by imitation or psychological suggestion. The novel does not explain their mechanism or confirm the custodians' ancestor theory.
There is no phone service at the shrine. The only link to the outside is the custodians' radio, which receives but does not clearly transmit.

power_structure.mechanism: Traditional authority as head custodian; controls access to food stores, sleeping assignments, and the shrine's daily schedule.
Ritual authority — she conducts the naming, asks the questions, determines when a declaration is sufficient.
Patriarchal family authority; commands through expectation and precedent. His power erodes as Arash diverges.

power_structure.over: No one formally; moral authority among families
power_structure.mechanism: Articulate political argument; his influence is persuasive, not structural, and fails against the custodians' material leverage.

power_structure.over: No one formally; emerges as a mediating figure
power_structure.mechanism: Competence, practical credibility, and the trust she earns from multiple families. Her authority is consensual, not imposed.

objects.name: Custodians' radio
objects.status: Functional but reception is intermittent; the primary link to outside news

objects.name: Leni's journal
objects.status: Written in nightly; never shown directly to the reader but its existence is referenced

objects.name: Peyam and Kal's shared pipe
objects.status: Shared with Kal Dao during the communal meal; a small symbol of cross-family bonding

objects.name: Bowl of cashew paste
objects.origin: Contributed by the Dao family from their provisions
objects.status: Triggers Melie's allergic reaction; must be tracked as a food safety concern for the rest of the novel

knowledge_state.knows: His wife is alive — she left, she did not die
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Everyone, especially Melie. Partially exposed in chapter_10, confessed to Suna in chapter_16, declared in chapter_18.

knowledge_state.knows: Her husband left the family three months ago — he did not go ahead
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Possibly known or suspected by Deshi. Hidden from children. Declared in chapter_18.

knowledge_state.knows: Fen is not her biological grandson — she took him as an infant from a dead family on the road 16 years ago
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Everyone — Fen, Miro, Enn, all other families. Confessed in chapter_14.

knowledge_state.knows: Her first child Ren drowned at age 3, seven years before the story
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Known to Hala. Hidden from general knowledge until chapter_13 when Venn's confrontation forces it into the open.

knowledge_state.knows: She and Bram had a son named Efe who died at three months, before Tomas was born
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Known to Bram. Hidden from Tomas and Leni. Revealed to Suna in chapter_08. Declared publicly in chapter_18.

knowledge_state.knows: He believes the flood will not recede until the naming is completed — the shrine holds its guests until the truth is spoken
knowledge_state.hidden_from: All families until chapter_12. Shared with Suna and Bram.

knowledge_state.knows: Arash Kondo and Bett Dao have a nighttime connection that crosses family lines
knowledge_state.hidden_from: All adults. Leni observes but does not report.

knowledge_state.knows: The manifestation pattern connects families through absence — each child fills a gap left by loss
knowledge_state.hidden_from: All families. Shared only with Olet. Not presented to the families as a theory — it informs Fasa's approach to the final naming but is not explicitly stated.

environmental_constants: The sound of the flood: a steady rush from the valley below, intensifying during rain, always audible from the shrine. The most persistent ambient sound.
Stone surfaces: the shrine is stone throughout — cool underfoot at night, warm during afternoon sun. The texture of stone under hands, feet, and sleeping mats is constant.
Cooking smoke: the kitchen fire produces a wood-smoke smell that permeates clothing and hair. Everyone at the shrine smells of smoke after the first few days.

Rain: intermittent heavy monsoon rain for most of the novel's timeline. The sound of rain on stone, the humidity after rain, the green smell of wet vegetation. The rain stops in Chapter 16 — its absence is as notable as its presence was.

Oil lamp light: warm, flickering, producing shadows in the main hall and sleeping quarters. The quality of light is distinctive — faces are seen in lamp-glow, not daylight, during evening and night scenes.
Proximity of bodies: 48 people in a compact compound. The smell of other people, the sound of breathing and snoring at night, the impossibility of privacy. Baby Ada's crying is heard by everyone.
Insects: mosquitoes at night, moths circling the oil lamps, the background hum of monsoon-season insects.
The visible waterline: from the main hall's open side, the flood is visible below — brown water covering the valley floor and the lower road. This view is referenced whenever characters look outward.
The custodians' bell: rung for naming ceremonies, meals, and the daily schedule. A specific sound that everyone at the shrine associates with obligation.
